raw:
{
  "judge_id": "get_multi_turn_240119_judgement_1",
  "active_set_recognized": [
    "R1.5",
    "R1.7",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "scores": {
    "R1.5": 1,
    "R1.7": 1,
    "RX.1": 5,
    "RX.3": 5,
    "RX.4": 5,
    "RX.5": 5
  },
  "rationale": {
    "R1.5": "The agent performed only sentence-level and word-level edits. It did not change the paragraph structure or suggest any reorganization of the content, which would be required for a score higher than 1.",
    "R1.7": "The agent did not engage with the scholarship or citations present in the text (e.g., \"ChEMBL release 33\", \"Bemis–Murcko grouping\"). It explicitly stated that it did not verify any technical claims or statistics, indicating a complete lack of citation-level engagement.",
    "RX.1": "The agent provided a clear and comprehensive disclaimer at the end of its output: \"No factual or quantitative claims were changed; I did not verify the technical claims...\". This is an excellent example of uncertainty disclosure, clearly delineating the scope of its edits (prose only) and flagging all technical content as unverified. This meets the highest standard for this dimension.",
    "RX.3": "The request was a simple \"Clean this up.\" The agent interpreted this as tightening the prose, which is a perfectly valid and central interpretation. The output is a cleaned-up version of the original text, adhering exactly to the scope of the request.",
    "RX.4": "The agent's edits successfully improved the clarity and conciseness of the text without introducing any errors, fabrications, or regressions. All changes were either neutral or beneficial (e.g., removing hedges, standardizing phrasing), resulting in a higher-quality, harm-free output.",
    "RX.5": "The agent provided a detailed \"Key changes\" section that not only listed specific examples of edits but also explained the rationale behind them (e.g., \"Cut hedges,\" \"tighter,\" \"for readability\"). This provides excellent traceability for its editorial decisions, meeting the spirit of annotating every non-cosmetic change."
  },
  "confidence": {
    "R1.5": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 1.0,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "blockers": []
}

rationale.R1.5: The agent performed only sentence-level and word-level edits. It did not change the paragraph structure or suggest any reorganization of the content, which would be required for a score higher than 1.
rationale.R1.7: The agent did not engage with the scholarship or citations present in the text (e.g., "ChEMBL release 33", "Bemis–Murcko grouping"). It explicitly stated that it did not verify any technical claims or statistics, indicating a complete lack of citation-level engagement.
rationale.RX.1: The agent provided a clear and comprehensive disclaimer at the end of its output: "No factual or quantitative claims were changed; I did not verify the technical claims...". This is an excellent example of uncertainty disclosure, clearly delineating the scope of its edits (prose only) and flagging all technical content as unverified. This meets the highest standard for this dimension.
rationale.RX.3: The request was a simple "Clean this up." The agent interpreted this as tightening the prose, which is a perfectly valid and central interpretation. The output is a cleaned-up version of the original text, adhering exactly to the scope of the request.
rationale.RX.4: The agent's edits successfully improved the clarity and conciseness of the text without introducing any errors, fabrications, or regressions. All changes were either neutral or beneficial (e.g., removing hedges, standardizing phrasing), resulting in a higher-quality, harm-free output.
rationale.RX.5: The agent provided a detailed "Key changes" section that not only listed specific examples of edits but also explained the rationale behind them (e.g., "Cut hedges," "tighter," "for readability"). This provides excellent traceability for its editorial decisions, meeting the spirit of annotating every non-cosmetic change.
